column1: # SIPRI Military Expenditure Database indicator catalog
# License: free academic with attribution; cite the dataset version.
# This file is the single source of truth for which SIPRI milex indicators
# are read in Stage 2. Every Stage 2 SIPRI milex adapter call resolves its
# raw_column from this file. Adding/removing an indicator here is the only
# change needed to widen or narrow the SIPRI milex ingestion for the prototype.
# The SIPRI xlsx uses three missing-value tokens in year cells:
#   "..."  — data unavailable for this country/year (the measurement exists
#            but SIPRI does not have it). Treated as NULL in normalized_value;
#            raw_value preserves the literal "..." for the audit trail.
#   "xxx"  — country did not exist or was not independent during this year

column2: Ukraine in 1918). Treated as NULL in

column1: #            normalized_value; raw_value preserves the literal "xxx" so a
#   ""     — empty cell (rare; appears in some region/sub-region rows and
#            occasionally in country rows where SIPRI has not yet released a
#            number). Treated as NULL in normalized_value; raw_value
# The Stage 2 adapter coerces all three to Python None / pandas NaN in the
# wide frame and to NULL in source_observations.normalized_value.
# The SIPRI xlsx data rows interleave region/sub-region labels with country
# names. The following labels are NOT countries and are filtered out by the
# covers all 16 labels found in the live xlsx.
# The xlsx header row position varies per sheet (the Stage 2 adapter detects
# it dynamically by scanning for the first row where col 0 = "Country"):
#   Share of GDP                → header at row 6
#   Share of Govt. spending    → header at row 8
# The fixture mirrors this per-sheet layout.

column8: Military expenditure as a percentage of gross domestic product. Most-cited SIPRI milex indicator; captures the share of the economy devoted to military spending. Cross-validates UCDP state-based indicators (more spending = more conflict preparedness). Higher = more militarized economy = worse peace signal.
Military expenditure per capita in current US$. Normalizes spending to population size; comparable across countries of different sizes. Higher = more military burden per citizen = worse peace signal.
Military expenditure in millions of constant (2024) US$. Real-terms scale
column9: cross-time comparable. Captures absolute level of military investment. Higher = larger military establishment = worse peace signal.

column8: Military expenditure as a percentage of total government spending. Captures the political priority of military spending within the fiscal envelope. Higher = more military in the budget = worse peace signal.
